examples.title: Pseudocode: YANG Data Model Structure
examples.description: Demonstrates the core YANG data modeling constructs including modules, containers, leaves, and lists
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Define_YANG_Module
INPUT: Module name, namespace, data definitions
OUTPUT: YANG module structure

BEGIN
  // Define YANG Module Structure
  MODULE temperature-sensor
    NAMESPACE "http://example.com/sensors"
    PREFIX "temp"
    ORGANIZATION "IoT Solutions Inc."
    REVISION "2024-01-01"

    // Container for device configuration
    CONTAINER device-config
      DESCRIPTION "Configuration parameters for sensor"
      
      LEAF device-id
        TYPE string
        MANDATORY true
        DESCRIPTION "Unique device identifier"
      
      LEAF sampling-interval
        TYPE uint32
        UNITS "seconds"
        DEFAULT 60
        DESCRIPTION "Reading interval"
      
      LEAF enabled
        TYPE boolean
        DEFAULT true
        DESCRIPTION "Device enable status"
    
    // Container for operational state (read-only)
    CONTAINER sensor-state
      CONFIG false
      DESCRIPTION "Current sensor readings"
      
      LEAF temperature
        TYPE decimal64
        FRACTION-DIGITS 2
        UNITS "celsius"
        DESCRIPTION "Current temperature"
      
      LEAF last-update
        TYPE date-and-time
        DESCRIPTION "Timestamp of last reading"
      
      LEAF battery-level
        TYPE uint8
        RANGE 0..100
        DESCRIPTION "Battery percentage"
    
    // List for historical readings
    LIST reading-history
      KEY "timestamp"
      DESCRIPTION "Historical temperature data"
      
      LEAF timestamp
        TYPE date-and-time
      
      LEAF temperature
        TYPE decimal64
        FRACTION-DIGITS 2
      
      LEAF humidity
        TYPE decimal64
        FRACTION-DIGITS 2

  RETURN Module structure with all definitions
END

ALGORITHM Validate_YANG_Data
INPUT: YANG model, data instance
OUTPUT: Validation result

BEGIN
  FOR each leaf in model
    CHECK type matches
    IF has range THEN
      CHECK value within range
    IF has pattern THEN
      CHECK matches regex
    IF mandatory THEN
      CHECK value present
  RETURN validation status
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the core YANG modeling constructs: modules define the namespace and organization, containers group related data, leaves represent single values, and lists represent sequences of entries. The distinction between config=true (writable) and config=false (read-only state) is shown. This is the conceptual foundation before writing actual YANG code.

examples.title: Python: YANG-Like Data Model Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation demonstrating YANG data modeling concepts with validation
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates YANG data modeling concepts programmatically. It shows: (1) YANG type definitions with validation (StringType, UInt32Type, Decimal64Type, BooleanType), (2) YANGLeaf for single values with mandatory flags and defaults, (3) YANGContainer for grouping nodes with config=true/false distinction, and (4) validation logic similar to what a NETCONF server performs. The create_iot_sensor_model() function mirrors the YANG module structure from the lecture.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of leaf nodes in the model
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing model structure and validation state

examples.title: C: YANG Data Structure Definitions
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating YANG-like data structures for IoT device management
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates YANG data modeling concepts using C structures. It shows: (1) YANGLeaf structure representing single values with type, mandatory flag, and config/state distinction, (2) YANGContainer for grouping related nodes, (3) YANGList for sequences, (4) IoTDeviceConfig and SensorState as concrete implementations, (5) validation functions that check constraints, and (6) NETCONF-like XML output format. The code illustrates how YANG models map to implementation data structures in network management systems.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for validation where n is number of fields
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing container and leaf structures
